statement.markdown: Solve the problem and fix the bugs.
statement.inputFormat: The input will consist of an integer denoting the size of the array, followed by that many space-separated integers.
statement.outputFormat: Print 'YES' if the array can be reconstructed from a stack, otherwise print 'NO'.
statement.notes: The array elements are distinct and positive.

examples.explanation: Yes because the elements can be reconstructed from a stack
No because there are duplicate elements in the array

hints.text: First hint text: Implement a stack and use a boolean flag to track whether the stack is valid.

editorial.title: Stack Implementation
editorial.intuition: We can solve this problem by maintaining an empty stack and checking if each element can be popped from a stack.
editorial.bruteForce: Brute force approach: Iterate over each element and check if the stack is valid after popping each element.
editorial.betterSolution: Better solution: Iterate over each element and maintain a stack; if an element cannot be pushed onto the stack, pop elements from the stack until it can be pushed or the stack is empty.
editorial.optimalSolution: Optimal solution: Iterate over each element and maintain a stack; if an element cannot be pushed onto the stack, pop elements from the stack until it can be pushed or the stack is empty. If the stack becomes empty, the input is invalid.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: Correctness proof: The optimal solution maintains a valid stack and returns 'YES' or 'NO' based on the input's validity.
editorial.dryRun: Step-by-step example run: Push 5 onto the stack, then pop it; push 4 onto the stack, then pop it; push 3 onto the stack, then pop it; push 2 onto the stack, then pop it; push 1 onto the stack, then pop it.
commonMistakes: Not popping elements from the stack correctly
